title: TSMC (TSM.US) Earnings Call: This year's capital expenditure is targeted at the upper end of the US$52-56 billion range; Middle East crisis may impact profitability. - 富途牛牛
content: The company forecasts second-quarter revenue to be between USD 39 billion and USD 40.2 billion, significantly higher than the USD 30.1 billion recorded in the same period last year and USD 35.9 billion in the first quarter. On the 16th, Taiwan Semiconductor held its Q1 2026 earnings call. Strong AI demand propelled the company's second-quarter revenue forecast to a record high. Taiwan Semiconductor's CEO C.C. Wei stated during the earnings analyst conference call, "AI demand is extremely robust," noting that the world is amidst an 'AI megatrend.' He also indicated that the company’s capital expenditure for this year will be at the higher end of the previously guided range—a statement likely to alleviate investor concerns about the impact of Middle East tensions on AI chip demand. In terms of overseas expansion, Taiwan Semiconductor is investing USD 165 billion to build a chip factory in Arizona, USA. Additionally, the company has revised its manufacturing plans in Japan, where it will produce 3-nanometer chips instead of the previously planned mature process nodes. Taiwan Semiconductor’s Q1 2026 results show net profit surged 58% year-over-year to TWD 572.5 billion (approximately USD 18.2 billion), surpassing market expectations, marking the company's eighth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth. The company forecasts second-quarter revenue to be between USD 39 billion and USD 40.2 billion, significantly higher than the USD 30.1 billion recorded in the same period last year and USD 35.9 billion in the first quarter. The war in the Middle East poses a potential threat to the supply of semiconductor production materials—key raw materials such as helium and hydrogen face supply chain disruption risks. Taiwan Semiconductor stated that it has already built up sufficient safety stock to address possible supply fluctuations. Prior to the earnings release, Taiwan Semiconductor's Taipei-listed shares closed at a record high of TWD 2,085, up 0.2%, with a year-to-date gain of 35%, outperforming Taiwan's weighted index, which rose 28% over the same period. The company's current market capitalization is approximately USD 1.7 trillion, nearly twice that of South Korea's Samsung Electronics. The company expects second-quarter sales to be between USD 39 billion and USD 40.2 billion, compared to the market estimate of USD 38.11 billion. It forecasts a second-quarter gross margin of 65.5% to 67.5%, versus the market estimate of 64.1%. Operating profit margin is expected to be 56.5%-58.5%. The company noted that the crisis in the Middle East could impact profitability. Helium and hydrogen procurement comes from multiple regions. It is expected that raw material supplies will not be affected in the short term. Capital expenditure for 2026 will approach the higher end of the USD 52 billion to USD 56 billion range. Sales in 2026 (in USD) are projected to grow by more than 30%. In light of the situation in the Middle East, business planning is being conducted cautiously. Demand for artificial intelligence remains 'extremely strong.' Customers have conveyed very strong demand signals. Production capacity remains tight. Attention is being paid to the impact of rising component prices. N2 process technology has entered mass production. To meet strong AI demand, the company is increasing capital expenditure to boost N3 capacity. It is expanding mature process capacity in Japan and Germany. Plans are underway to gradually phase out 6-inch wafer fabs. The company will continue optimizing its capacity strategy. Customers are highly focused on the A14 process, with mass production expected to begin in 2028. Intel and Tesla are both our customers and competitors, with Intel being a very strong competitor. We understand that the technologies offered by competitors are highly attractive. We are confident in our technological position. A CoWoS pilot production line is under construction. We have strong confidence in the super trend of AI, and capital expenditure over the next three years will be significantly higher than the past three years. We are preparing capacity to meet customer demand. Rising memory prices have had a certain impact on the personal computer and smartphone markets. Efforts are being made to accelerate the construction progress of our factory in Arizona, USA, with hopes of building more wafer fabs in Arizona. The following is the full transcript of the conference call: Jeff Su, Director of Investor Relations: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to Taiwan Semiconductor's First Quarter 2026 Earnings Conference Call. I am Jeff Su, Director of Investor Relations at Taiwan Semiconductor, and the host of today’s meeting. Today’s agenda is as follows: First, Mr. Wendell Huang, Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer of Taiwan Semiconductor, will summarize our operational performance for the first quarter of 2026, followed by guidance for the second quarter of 2026. Afterward, Mr. Huang and Dr. C.C. Wei, Chairman and CEO of Taiwan Semiconductor, will jointly communicate the company’s key messages. Then we will move into the Q&A session. As usual, I would like to remind everyone that today’s discussion may contain forward-looking statements, which are subject to significant risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially from those in the forward-looking statements. Therefore, please refer to the Safe Harbor statement in our press release. Now, I would like to turn the floor over to Taiwan Semiconductor’s CFO, Mr. Wendell Huang, who will summarize the operations and provide guidance for this quarter. Wendell Huang, Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer: Thank you, Jeff. Good afternoon, and thank you for joining us today. My presentation will begin with the financial highlights of the first quarter of 2026, followed by guidance for the second quarter of 2026. Driven by robust demand for our advanced process technologies, revenue in the first quarter grew by 8.4% sequentially in New Taiwan Dollar terms. In US Dollar terms, revenue increased by 6.4% sequentially to reach $35.9 billion, slightly above our guidance for the first quarter. Gross margin improved by 3.9 percentage points sequentially to 66.2%, primarily due to cost improvement efforts, high capacity utilization, and favorable exchange rates. Operating margin increased by 4.1 percentage points sequentially to 58.1%, benefiting from operating leverage. Overall, our earnings per share (EPS) for the first quarter was NT$22.08, and return on equity (ROE) was 40.5%. Now let’s look at revenue by technology node. Our 3-nanometer process technology contributed 25% of wafer revenue in the first quarter, while 5-nanometer and 7-nanometer accounted for 36% and 13%, respectively. Advanced processes (defined as 7-nanometer and below) represented 74% of total wafer revenue. Revenue contribution by platform: High-performance computing (HPC) grew by 20% sequentially, accounting for 61% of first-quarter revenue; smartphones declined by 11%, contributing 26%; Internet of Things (IoT) grew by 12%, contributing 6%; automotive declined by 7%, contributing 4%; digital consumer electronics (DCE) grew by 28%, contributing 1%. On the balance sheet, our cash and marketable securities amounted to TWD 3.4 trillion (approximately USD 106 billion) at the end of the first quarter. On the liabilities side, current liabilities increased by TWD 256 billion quarter-over-quarter, primarily due to an increase of TWD 129 billion in accrued liabilities and other payables, as well as an increase of TWD 82 billion in accounts payable. Regarding financial ratios, days sales outstanding remained flat at 26 days. Inventory turnover days increased by 6 days to reach 80 days, reflecting the capacity ramp-up of our 2-nanometer technology and strong demand for 3-nanometer technology. Regarding cash flow and capital expenditure (CapEx): In the first quarter, we generated approximately TWD 699 billion in cash from operations, with capital expenditures amounting to TWD 351 billion, and distributed a cash dividend of TWD 130 billion for the second quarter of 2025. Overall, our cash balance increased by TWD 268 billion, reaching TWD 3 trillion at the end of the quarter. In dollar terms, our total capital expenditure for the first quarter was USD 11.1 billion. This concludes the financial summary. Let us now turn to the guidance for this quarter. Based on the current business outlook, we expect second-quarter revenue to be between USD 39 billion and USD 40.2 billion. At the midpoint, this represents a quarter-over-quarter growth of 10% or a year-over-year growth of 32%. Assuming an exchange rate of TWD 31.7 to USD 1, the gross margin is expected to range between 65.5% and 67.5%, while the operating margin is projected to be between 56.5% and 58.5%. Additionally, we need to account for unallocated profit tax in the second quarter, resulting in an estimated tax rate of approximately 20% for Q2. We continue to forecast the full-year tax rate to be between 17% and 18%. This concludes my financial presentation. Let me now discuss key highlights. I will first address our profitability for the first and second quarters of 2026. Compared to the fourth quarter, our gross margin in the first quarter increased by 390 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 66.2%, primarily driven by cost improvements, higher overall capacity utilization, and more favorable exchange rates. Compared to our first-quarter guidance, the actual gross margin exceeded the upper limit of the range provided three months ago by 120 basis points. The midpoint of the second-quarter gross margin guidance we just provided reflects a quarter-over-quarter increase of 30 basis points to 66.5%, mainly propelled by improved overall utilization and ongoing cost reductions (including productivity gains), but partially offset by the dilutive effects of overseas fab expansions. Looking ahead to the second half of the year, there will be some fluctuations in the six factors that determine our profitability. As previously mentioned, the initial ramp-up of our 2-nanometer (N2) technology will begin to dilute our gross margin in the second half of this year, with an expected impact of 2% to 3% for the full year of 2026. Moreover, as the scale of our overseas expansion increases, we continue to project that the gross margin dilution caused by overseas fab ramp-ups will be in the range of 2% to 3% during the initial phase and expand to 3% to 4% in later stages over the next few years. Additionally, given the recent developments in the Middle East, prices of certain chemicals and gases may rise. Based on our current assessment, this could impact profitability, but it is still too early to quantify the effect. On the other hand, we will continue leveraging our superior manufacturing capabilities to produce more wafers and drive better cross-node capacity optimization within our fabs to support our profitability. Meanwhile, the gross margin for 3-nanometer (N3) technology is expected to exceed the company average in the second half of 2026. Finally, exchange rates, which are beyond our control, may also play a role. Next, let’s address material and energy supply conditions given the recent situation in the Middle East. Taiwan Semiconductor operates a robust enterprise risk management system. In terms of material supply, our strategy is to continuously develop multi-source supply solutions. For specialty chemicals and gases, including helium and hydrogen, we procure from multiple suppliers across different regions and maintain safety stock. Therefore, we do not anticipate any impact on near-term operations from material supply. Regarding energy, Taiwan Semiconductor collaborates closely with Taipower and the authorities in Taiwan Province to ensure stable and sufficient energy supply. The authorities in Taiwan Province have announced securing adequate LNG (liquefied natural gas) supply until at least May and are actively pursuing additional supplies, diversifying procurement to other regions, and preparing backup power plants. Thus, we expect no disruptions to near-term operations. Finally, let’s discuss Taiwan Semiconductor's capital expenditure budget for 2026. Higher levels of capital expenditure are consistently linked to greater growth opportunities in the coming years. With a strong technological leadership position and differentiation, we are well-positioned to capture multi-year structural demand driven by industry megatrends such as 5G, AI, and HPC. We now expect the capital expenditure budget for 2026 to approach the upper end of our USD 52 billion to USD 56 billion range, as we continue to make significant investments to support our customers’ growth. Even at this level of capital expenditure, we remain committed to delivering profitable growth for shareholders and ensuring sustainable and steady growth in cash dividends per share on both an annual and quarterly basis. Now, I will pass the microphone to C.C. (C. C. Wei). C. C. Wei, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer: Thank you, Wendell. Good afternoon, everyone. First, let me start with the near-term demand outlook. Our revenue in the first quarter was USD 35.9 billion, slightly above our guidance in dollar terms, driven by robust demand for advanced process technologies. Entering the second quarter of 2026, we expect our business to continue being supported by strong demand for advanced process technologies. Looking ahead, we are paying close attention to the impact of rising component prices, especially in consumer and price-sensitive end markets. Additionally, recent developments in the Middle East have introduced further macroeconomic uncertainty. Therefore, we are maintaining a cautious approach in our business planning. That said, demand related to AI remains extremely strong. The shift from generative AI (query-based models) to agentic AI (instruction and action-based models) has triggered another surge in token consumption. This has driven increasing demand for computational power, thereby supporting robust demand for the most advanced chips. Our customers, as well as their customers (primarily cloud service providers), continue to send us very strong signals and positive outlooks. Therefore, our confidence in the multi-year megatrend of AI remains high. We now confidently maintain our expectation that full-year 2026 revenue will grow by more than 30% in dollar terms. Next, I will discuss our N2 (2-nanometer) capacity expansion plan. Our usual practice is to prioritize the use of land in Taiwan Province to support the rapid ramp-up of the latest nodes, as today’s operations require close integration with R&D. Our new node, N2, has entered high-volume manufacturing (HVM) in the fourth quarter of 2025, with good yields. Driven by robust demand from smartphone and HPC/AI applications, N2 is successfully ramping up in multiple phases at our Hsinchu and Kaohsiung facilities. With our continuously enhanced strategies (such as N2P and A16), we expect the N2 family to become another large and long-lasting node for Taiwan Semiconductor. Now let me discuss Taiwan Semiconductor's global N3 capacity expansion plan. Historically, once a node reaches its target capacity, we typically do not add extra capacity. However, as a foundry, our primary responsibility is to provide customers with the most advanced technology and necessary capacity. To meet the strong demand in AI applications, we are increasing capital expenditure investments to expand N3 capacity. Therefore, we are implementing a global capacity plan to support multi-year strong demand for 3-nanometer technology from customers in smartphones, HPC, AI (including HBM-based die), automotive, and IoT sectors. In Taiwan Province: We are adding a new 3-nanometer fab to the GIGAFAB cluster in the Tainan Science Park, with plans to begin mass production in the first half of 2027. In Arizona, USA: Our second fab will also adopt 3-nanometer technology. Construction has been completed, and mass production is scheduled to start in the second half of 2027. In Japan: We now plan to adopt 3-nanometer technology in our second fab, with mass production scheduled for 2028. In addition to all the new fabs, we are continuing to convert 5-nanometer equipment in Taiwan Province to support 3-nanometer capacity. We are also focused on cross-node capacity optimization, including providing flexible capacity support between the N7, N5, and N3 nodes. We want to emphasize that in situations of tight capacity, we will not discriminate or show favoritism among customers. Next, let me discuss our mature node strategy. Our strategy for mature nodes remains unchanged, focusing on building high-yield capacity for specialty technologies. For example, we are increasing mature node capacity, such as JASM in Japan (for CMOS image sensor applications) and ESMC in Germany (for automotive and industrial applications). At the same time, we plan to gradually phase out Fab 2 (6-inch fab) and Fab 5 (8-inch fab), shifting focus to gallium nitride (GaN) and optimizing available space to better support leading-edge applications. Even without Fab 2 and Fab 5, we still have sufficient capacity to fully support existing customers. Finally, let me discuss the status of our A14. A14 adopts our second-generation nanosheet transistor structure, enabling another full node leap starting from N2, offering performance and power efficiency advantages to meet insatiable demand for high-performance and energy-efficient computing. Compared to N2, A14 will provide a 10% to 15% speed improvement at the same power level, or a 25% to 30% power consumption reduction at the same speed, along with an almost 20% increase in chip density. The development of A14 technology is proceeding smoothly. We are observing significant interest and engagement from customers in smartphone and HPC applications. A14 is scheduled for mass production in 2028. This concludes our key messages. Thank you all for your attention. Host: The first questioner is Brad Lin from Bank of America. Anonymous Participant: Good afternoon. C.C., Wendell, Jeff, good afternoon. Congratulations on your impressive results and guidance, and thank you for taking my question. I would like to start with the outlook for the gross margin of the 3-nanometer node. You mentioned earlier that the gross margin for this node will exceed the company’s overall average—currently around 65%—in the second half of this year. We also understand that due to strong AI demand, this technology node is currently in severe undersupply, and your company has forecasted continued capacity expansion through conversions and new builds until 2028. Could you elaborate further on which applications are driving such strong business demand and giving your company confidence to continue expanding? Additionally, regarding the 3-nanometer node, which began mass production in the fourth quarter of 2022, this means some equipment will be fully depreciated by around 2027. Against the backdrop of high utilization rates and stable pricing trends, should we expect the gross margin for this node to rise further? Thank you. Jeff Su (Director of Investor Relations): Alright, the first question from Brad Lin at Bank of America consists of two parts, both centered around the 3-nanometer process. The first part relates to what C.C. described—our ongoing capacity expansion plan for 3-nanometer technology. He wants to understand which applications are driving a robust multi-year demand pipeline for 3-nanometer—considering this node has been in mass production since late 2022. That is the first part of the question. Wendell Huang (Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer): Let me address that. I think the answer is straightforward: it’s HPC and AI applications. Does that resolve your query? Anonymous Participant: Okay, that was the first part; the second part might be more... Jeff Su: The second part of the question pertains to the gross margin for the 3-nanometer process. Specifically, he is asking about the gross margin outlook for 3-nanometer: will it surpass the company's overall level in the second half of this year? If so, what level will it reach? And once fully depreciated, how will the gross margin change? Wendell Huang: Alright, this is Wendell. We expect the N3 gross margin to reach and exceed the company’s overall gross margin level in the second half of this year. Specific figures cannot be disclosed at this time. However, based on our experience with previous nodes, the gross margin tends to be very high once fully depreciated. Jeff Su: Alright, I’ll count that as 1.5 questions. If you have a brief follow-up on the second question, please proceed. Anonymous Participant: Thank you, Jeff. The additional half-question concerns capital expenditure. Compared to three months ago, you raised the guidance for this year’s capital expenditure to the upper end of the $52 billion to $56 billion range. After communicating with customers and their downstream clients, what factors have bolstered your confidence in the demand outlook, supporting the upward adjustment of this year’s capital expenditure guidance? Jeff Su: Thank you. His second question highlights that compared to January, we indeed adjusted the capital expenditure guidance to the higher end this time. What incremental factors drove this adjustment? What gives us the confidence to set the capital expenditure guidance at the upper end of the $52 billion to $56 billion range? C. C. Wei (Chairman and Chief Executive Officer): Alright, let me answer that. The answer is simple: demand is extremely strong, particularly driven by HPC and AI applications. At the same time, we are making every effort to accelerate the construction pace and procure equipment earlier than planned. Even so, our supply remains very tight while demand continues to rise. Therefore, we are working closely with suppliers to expedite progress, which is why we moved the capital expenditure guidance to the higher end. Anonymous participant: Yes, thank you very much. I will return to the queue to wait for my turn to ask questions. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you. Host, please proceed to the next questioner. Host: The next questioner is Gokul Hariharan from JPMorgan. Gokul Hariharan (Analyst): Good afternoon, C.C., Wendell, and Jeff. My first question pertains to demand. C.C., demand clearly appears stronger than what you projected in January, and you have also increased your capital expenditure. All clients seem to be indicating that wafers remain the biggest bottleneck. Given that your company has expanded its 3-nanometer capacity plan and accelerated capital spending, how long do you anticipate the supply constraints will persist? Based on discussions with clients, do you have any forecast regarding when supply and demand might reach equilibrium? Additionally, from a strategic perspective, does your company also plan to prioritize the expansion of cleanroom facilities, as this seems to be a current bottleneck in rapidly scaling production capacity? That is my first question. Jeff Su: Okay, Gokul, allow me to summarize your first question. His inquiry is directed at C.C.: Demand appears stronger than our projections in January, we have raised our capital expenditure, and clients continue to express the need for more chip supply. Based on our capacity plans, do we have any forecast on how long the supply tightness will last? Will we prioritize expanding cleanroom facilities? Please confirm if my understanding is correct, Gokul. C. C. Wei: Alright, Gokul, I’ll answer again, and the response remains straightforward. Demand continues to surge with rising figures. We have carefully verified this with clients—including their end customers, such as cloud service providers (CSPs)—and they have provided highly optimistic forecasts. Therefore, we must expedite the pace of cleanroom construction and equipment procurement, working closely with contractors and equipment suppliers to bring forward the original schedule. Ultimately, the reason is the overwhelming demand driven by AI. Gokul Hariharan: C.C., do you have any judgment on when these demands can be met? Or will it remain extremely challenging over the next few years—with supply potentially still falling short of demand until 2027? Jeff Su: Gokul wants to know when supply can meet demand and whether we have any specific forecast or timeline? C.C. Wei: Gokul, building a new wafer fab takes two to three years. According to the current plan, we believe that by 2027... when we enter 2027, there will naturally be announcements. But in general, building a new fab takes time, and ramping up to full production also takes time, so supply will remain very tight. This is precisely why we announced the construction of three new wafer fabs to meet demand. Gokul Hariharan: Understood, very clear — still tight in 2027. The second question relates to competition. In addition to traditional competitors, one of your clients, Elon Musk, recently announced the 'Tera Fab' initiative. What is Taiwan Semiconductor's view on this? They were previously a client of Taiwan Semiconductor and signed a cooperation agreement with Samsung a few months ago. How does Taiwan Semiconductor respond to their attempt to build their own chip manufacturing capabilities? How do you aim to win back this client? Jeff Su: Alright, Gokul’s second question is about competition. He noted that we are facing competition as well as the recent emergence of the 'Tera Fab' initiative. He wants to understand our perspective on this matter. This client was previously a customer of Taiwan Semiconductor but has also signed a cooperation agreement with our competitor, Samsung. Gokul also hopes to learn our evaluation of Tera Fab and how we view the possibility of regaining this client’s business. C.C. Wei: Alright, Gokul, in fact, both Intel and Tesla are clients of Taiwan Semiconductor. Of course, they are also our competitors — we consider Intel a strong competitor and would never underestimate them. However, there are no shortcuts. The fundamental rules of the foundry industry have never changed: technological leadership, superior manufacturing capability, and customer trust, especially service — as Jensen mentioned, thank you for his acknowledgment. Let me reiterate that building a new fab takes two to three years, with no shortcuts, and then requires another one to two years to ramp up production. This is the basic rule of the foundry industry. As for whether we need to win them back — they are still our clients. We are highly confident in our technological capabilities and will do our best to secure every piece of business. Gokul Hariharan: Very clear. Then, do you think accelerating capacity expansion can help win back these clients? It seems that the reason for client diversion is mainly due to tight capacity rather than other significant factors. Is this your judgment — that capacity is the most critical factor in winning back clients? Jeff Su: Gokul’s final question is: regarding efforts to regain clients, his concern is whether tight capacity is indeed the reason we lost clients. If we increase capacity, can we win these clients back? C.C. Wei: Let me reiterate that building a new fab takes two to three years, so we are constructing new fabs to meet stronger client demand; there are no shortcuts. In summary, capacity is indeed extremely tight, but we are striving to ensure that we can meet client needs. Gokul Hariharan: Understood, thank you C.C. — no shortcuts, understood. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you. Host, please proceed to the next questioner. Host: The next questioner is Charlie Chan from Morgan Stanley. Charlie Chan: Good afternoon, C.C., Wendell, and Jeff. Thank you for taking my questions, and congratulations again on your company's outstanding results. I would like to discuss the topic of competition from a slightly different angle. We can see that AI customers are developing chips with increasingly larger reticle sizes, and some customers are considering using the EMIB solution because it is substrate-based and more suitable for large circular chip designs. I would like to understand Taiwan Semiconductor's strategy to address this competition and, from a broader strategic perspective, whether Taiwan Semiconductor is willing to open its front-end wafers to competitors (such as Intel) to complete the packaging? What are the considerations behind this? Thank you. Jeff Su: Alright, Charlie, thank you. Charlie’s first question is also related to competition. He noticed that AI customers have a demand for increasingly larger reticle sizes and would like to know how we assess the competitive threat from solutions such as EMIB and our response strategy — are we willing to open up our front-end wafers and let others handle the packaging? C.C. Wei: Alright, Charlie, Taiwan Semiconductor currently offers the world's largest reticle size packaging solution. We are also aware that competitors possess highly attractive technologies, and we welcome this — more choices for customers mean more business opportunities for us, which is our attitude. Of course, we will not easily give up any business opportunity and are working hard to meet all customer needs. We are also developing advanced packaging technologies for even larger sizes while maintaining collaboration with all customers, and progress is going smoothly. Charlie Chan: Thank you, C.C. A follow-up question: when you mentioned larger-size packaging technology, did you mean CoWoS, CoWoS-L, or the 3.5D solution? Do you think CDE stacking can solve the issue of planar scaling? Jeff Su: Charlie is asking for clarification on the larger reticle size. He wants us to specify: is it CoWoS-L, panel-level packaging, or what specific solution are we advancing? C.C. Wei: Charlie, currently, we primarily offer large-size CoWoS solutions. Of course, we are also advancing CoWoS-L and are committed to providing customers with sufficient capacity at a reasonable cost. This is why we are currently building our CoWoS production pipeline, which is expected to enter mass production in a few years. For now, however, the mainstream solution remains large-size CoWoS combined with System on Wafer technology, and we believe Taiwan Semiconductor can provide the best product solutions for our customers. Charlie Chan: Understood. So we don’t need to be overly concerned about competition at the packaging level. My second question is about long-term capital expenditure plans. C.C., you mentioned that it takes two to three years to build a new factory, so you must have a fairly clear visibility into the future. I remember that management provided guidance in 2021 for a three-year capital expenditure totaling $100 billion. Given the strong current demand, could Taiwan Semiconductor provide a slightly longer-term capital expenditure outlook? Additionally, equipment supply is quite tight — ASML reported very strong earnings yesterday. Has EUV supply become an issue? Is management considering providing investors with long-term capital expenditure guidance? Thank you. C. C. Wei: Alright, Charlie, there are many questions. Let’s address the second one regarding capital expenditure first. Jeff Su (relaying the question): Charlie understands that C. C. mentioned capacity cannot be built overnight and will take time. Therefore, he would like to know whether, in addition to this year’s already stated high-end capital expenditure, we can provide guidance on the total capital expenditure for the next three years, similar to what was done in 2021? C. C. Wei: Alright, Charlie, currently, we do not have specific figures to share. However, it can be understood as follows: Over the past three years, our total capital expenditure amounted to approximately NT$101 billion. This year’s capital expenditure has already been explained. We expect that the capital expenditure over the next few years, specifically the next three years, will significantly exceed that of the previous years. Jeff Su: The final part of Charlie’s question is: Given such long delivery cycles, are we concerned about procurement and supply bottlenecks for equipment? Wendell Huang: Alright, Charlie, Taiwan Semiconductor's consistent culture is to regard suppliers as partners and maintain deep cooperation with them, especially companies like ASML, Applied Materials, and Lam Research. Currently, their support for us is highly satisfactory, and that is all I can tell you at this moment. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you, Charlie. Host, please proceed to the next questioner. Anonymous Participant (Sunny): Good afternoon, thank you very much for taking my question, and congratulations on your company’s robust performance. My first question remains about capital expenditure. From 2024 to 2026, within this cloud computing cycle, Taiwan Semiconductor, leveraging its strong technological leadership and operating leverage, has maintained a healthy capital intensity level of around 30%—even though the company does not set an explicit target for capital intensity. Looking ahead, given the strong growth momentum of advanced process revenue, how should we view the relationship between revenue growth and capital expenditure growth? Can revenue grow steadily while capital expenditure grows in line with or below revenue growth? What is the best framework for understanding this? Wendell Huang: Alright, Sunny, thank you for your question. Allow me to summarize first… Jeff Su: Sunny's first question is about capital intensity. She noted that over the past few years, we have maintained our capital intensity at around 30%-plus and also acknowledged that we do not have a specific target set. However, her specific question is: looking ahead to the next few years, how will revenue growth compare to capital expenditure growth — higher, flat, or lower? What level of capital intensity does this correspond to? Please confirm if my understanding is correct, Sunny. C.C. Wei: Alright, Sunny. As you correctly pointed out, over the past few years, revenue growth has outpaced capital expenditure growth. If we continue to do our job well, this trend of revenue growth exceeding capital expenditure growth will persist in the coming years. Therefore, we do not expect a sudden significant increase in capital intensity in the next few years. Sunny: Understood. A quick follow-up question: from a competitive perspective, given the persistent tightness in supply in recent years, is Taiwan Semiconductor considering moderately increasing its capital expenditures to prevent customers from diversifying their risks due to insufficient supply? Jeff Su: Alright, Sunny’s follow-up question is: against the backdrop of competitive threats, would we consider increasing capital expenditures? If capacity is insufficient, customers might shift to competitors. Is that your question, correct? Wendell Huang: Alright, Sunny. We have repeatedly emphasized that we plan our capacity based on customer demand rather than in response to competition or other considerations. Customer demand remains the most important driver, and we work closely with our customers to plan capacity and capital expenditures accordingly. Have I answered your question? Sunny: Very clear, thank you. One additional half-question: earlier, you provided guidance indicating more than 30% revenue growth for the full year of 2025, but supply constraints are still ongoing. How much further upside can we expect for 2026? Are you currently observing any signs of weakness in consumer end markets — specifically in smartphones and PCs? Jeff Su: Alright, Sunny’s second question relates to the full-year outlook for 2026. She noted that we have raised our guidance to exceed 30%, and she wants to know how much upside potential remains. Additionally, what impact is memory price inflation having on end markets? Is there potential for even greater upside beyond the 'more than 30%' figure? C.C. Wei: Alright, Sunny. Memory price inflation is indeed having an impact on price-sensitive end markets, particularly in the PC and smartphone sectors, where we are seeing some softening. However, premium smartphones continue to perform well, which is beneficial for Taiwan Semiconductor. As for your question about how much upside potential remains beyond the 'over 30%,' we will share that with everyone in July, when we will have more accurate and precise figures. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you, Sunny. Moderator, please proceed to the next questioner. Anonymous Participant (Jim): Thank you for taking my question. My first question pertains to demand. Earlier during this conference call, you mentioned that demand for advanced process capacity continues to outstrip supply, and you've just announced robust earnings and gross margin guidance. Against this backdrop, has management’s perspective on sustainable profit margin structure and long-term return levels changed? Jeff Su: Alright, Jim's first question relates to the profit margin structure. He noted, as we stated, demand is extremely strong. So your question is, how does this alter our view of the long-term profit margin corridor and return levels? Please confirm if this understanding is correct. C.C. Wei: Alright, Jim, as we stated in the previous earnings call, we have raised our long-term profit margin target and shareholder equity return target, applicable from 2024 to 2029. We now anticipate that the gross margin will remain at 56% or above throughout this period, and ROE will stay in the high 20% range — which is already higher than the previous target. Jim: Thank you. In light of other segments of the supply chain clearly starting to record excess returns, does this judgment remain unchanged? Will this not affect your view of the profit margin structure over the next two to three years? Speaker: Yes, Jim, long-term planning is an ongoing dynamic process that we are always engaged in. If there are any changes, we will update you promptly. Jim: Okay, thank you. My second question is that the factory in Arizona seems to be becoming increasingly strategically significant in advanced process technology, especially after recently purchasing a second plot of land. Could you share the mid-to-long-term capacity expansion plan? Additionally, how confident are you that the economic efficiency of the U.S. factories can match the wafers produced in Taiwan Province, China? Speaker (Moderator): Alright, Jim’s second question pertains to the expansion plans for the Arizona factory. He noted that the plant is becoming increasingly strategic, and we have recently acquired a second large plot of land. What are the plans and objectives behind this? And what is the outlook for profitability or profit margins? C.C. Wei: Okay, Jim, let me address that. The reason we acquired the second plot of land is that we have a need—to build more factories in Arizona to meet multi-year demand from leading-edge process customers in the U.S. Again, we are making every effort to accelerate progress. We have accumulated substantial experience in Arizona, so we are more confident now than last year about making good progress. We will continue to push forward actively, and we expect the cost structure to improve continuously. Speaker: Okay, Jim, thank you. Host, please proceed to the next questioner. Host: The next questioner is Bruce Lu from Goldman Sachs. Bruce Lu (Analyst): Thank you for taking my question. I’d like to extend Jim’s inquiry on profitability. I recall asking last year why Taiwan Semiconductor hadn’t raised its profitability target despite consistently enhancing value, and C.C. directed me to focus on the guidance of '53% and above.' Last quarter, this target was revised upward to '56% and above.' My question is: Do you believe the current profitability level adequately reflects the value of Taiwan Semiconductor? I assume C.C. might again direct me to focus on the upper range of the profitability target. The real question is: Given Taiwan Semiconductor’s unique and dominant market position, it is difficult to find a perfect benchmark for profitability. How should we view the benchmark for Taiwan Semiconductor’s profitability? Or, what is the best way to fully reflect Taiwan Semiconductor’s value in terms of gross margin and operating margin? Jeff Su: Alright, Bruce’s first question pertains to profitability. He wants to know what benchmark we should use to measure Taiwan Semiconductor and whether we believe the current profitability level fully reflects the true value of Taiwan Semiconductor. C.C. Wei: Alright, Bruce, you’re essentially asking about our pricing strategy. Let me say that we always regard our customers as partners. We are certainly aware of our value and market position, but we also see our customers as very important business partners, so we do not make drastic changes to pricing. We simply strive to ensure that our customers succeed in the market, and we grow together by co-creating value, which allows us to continually expand capacity to support them. This is our fundamental consideration—first, our customers must succeed; second, we grow together. Remember one key term: Customers are our partners. Bruce: Understood. So if customers continue to succeed, perhaps in a few more quarters, we may see higher profitability targets. Bruce: Alright, the second question is: Management has consistently projected that AI accelerator revenue will grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) in the upper 50% range from 2024 to 2029. How does Taiwan Semiconductor plan and forecast AI-related demand? Have you incorporated indicators such as token consumption growth into your assumptions? Because token consumption in the first quarter clearly accelerated beyond earlier expectations. Has there been any change in the projection for AI accelerator revenue growth in the coming years? Jeff Su: Alright, Bruce’s second question pertains to our long-term CAGR guidance for AI accelerators — which we previously indicated to be in the upper 50% range. He has noticed strong growth in token demand and is asking whether we will adjust this long-term guidance. C.C. Wei: Bruce, the demand is indeed very strong, and we continue to receive highly positive signals from our customers — as well as their customers. Regarding your question about whether we will adjust the CAGR guidance for AI accelerators, we are indeed observing sustained strong demand. Let me be more specific: the CAGR we are currently observing tends to be in the high 30% range. Jeff Su: Thank you, C.C., and thank you, Bruce. Host, please proceed to the next questioner. Jeff Su: The next questioner is Laura Chen from Citi. Laura Chen (Analyst): Good afternoon, C.C., Wendell, Jeff. Could you provide further insights into Taiwan Semiconductor's strategy in advanced packaging and the business model with OSAT partners? We see various packaging solutions across the industry, with OSAT companies offering diverse options, while Taiwan Semiconductor is also expanding its advanced packaging operations. How does Taiwan Semiconductor coordinate frontend wafer demand planning with customers, and how do you align advanced packaging capacity needs on your platform? Jeff Su: Alright, thank you, Laura. Laura’s first question relates to advanced packaging. She wants to understand: how do we collaborate with customers to plan frontend wafer capacity, and how do we coordinate advanced packaging capacity planning with customers? Also, how does this model work in collaboration with OSAT partners? Wendell Huang: Our primary principle has always been to support our customers. At every step, we aim to ensure that customer product demands — including those for advanced packaging — can be met by Taiwan Semiconductor. Of course, our advanced packaging capacity is currently under significant strain, so we need to collaborate with OSAT partners to expand capacity and support customers. To reiterate, supporting our customers is our top priority. We are striving to increase our own capacity, but the current situation remains challenging. Laura Chen: Understood. My second question is also related to advanced packaging. C.C. has mentioned multiple times that AI chips are evolving into "superchips" with extremely large die sizes, and Taiwan Semiconductor is currently producing using the largest reticle size available globally. However, this also presents technical challenges, such as warpage issues. Do you believe that technology roadmaps like SoIC or CoWoS can address these types of technical difficulties? Based on Taiwan Semiconductor's technology roadmap, can these technologies achieve greater breakthroughs in the coming years? Spokesperson (Summary): Laura's second question also pertains to advanced packaging, AI, and larger reticle sizes—these trends introduce technical challenges such as warpage. She would like to understand our perspective on the role of SoIC or panel-level packaging in addressing these issues, where the key solutions lie, and the outlook for the coming years. C. C. Wei: Alright, Laura, very well put. The field of advanced packaging indeed faces significant challenges, including mechanical stress—a topic that is quite challenging for someone with my electrical engineering background (laughs). However, we have accumulated extensive hands-on experience because Taiwan Semiconductor is already a leading supplier in advanced packaging. We continuously increase wafer sizes and overcome various challenges such as mechanical stress, warpage, and thermal limitations. These are all positive challenges—the harder, the better, because this is where Taiwan Semiconductor’s technical engineering strength lies. We are confident in working with our customers to address all difficulties and keep advancing. Laura Chen: Understood. Should we expect SoIC to be rolled out earlier to tackle these challenges? Since Taiwan Semiconductor has already accumulated a learning curve and has products in mass production, it should progress faster than other technological approaches, right? Jeff Su: Laura’s question is very specific: what is the development path for SoIC? C. C. Wei: Alright, we collaborate closely with our customers to meet their needs—whether it involves speeding up or slowing down, everything is customer-driven. That’s all I can share with you. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you. Host, considering the time, please move to the final questioner. Host: The final questioner is Charles Shi from Needham. Charles Shi (Analyst): Thank you for taking my question. Taiwan Semiconductor's definition of AI revenue encompasses data center GPUs, AI accelerators, and HBM controllers, but explicitly excludes data center CPUs. This definition has been in use for several years. However, there has been increasing discussion recently indicating that CPUs are becoming a core component of AI infrastructure, particularly in the Agentic workload domain. Is it possible for Taiwan Semiconductor to adjust the definition of AI revenue to include data center CPUs and provide revised figures and growth forecasts? If data center CPUs used for Agentic AI workloads were included, how would historical AI revenue data change? That’s my first question. Jeff Su: Alright, thank you, Charles. To summarize: Charles's first question relates to the scope of our AI accelerator definition—currently including GPUs, ASICs, and HBM controllers used for training and inference in data centers. He noted that with the rise of Agentic AI, CPUs are becoming increasingly important, and he wants to know if we will start incorporating CPUs into this definition. If so, could we provide the historical data with CPUs included and corresponding AI accelerator growth guidance? C.C. Wei: Charles, CPUs are indeed becoming more critical in today’s AI data centers, and that’s a good question. Let me share this with you: the challenge is that we cannot distinguish whether a particular CPU is used for PCs, desktops, or AI data centers. Therefore, we still do not include CPUs in the calculation of AI/HPC revenue. At some point in the future, we may reconsider this issue. Host: Alright, Charles, do you have a second question? Charles Shi: Yes, this question is also related to recent trends in the evolution of AI infrastructure. In NVIDIA’s recently released Vera Rubin superchip, there is more CPU content, but most people are paying more attention to their newly launched LPU product. We understand that Taiwan Semiconductor has strong capabilities in the CPU field and will certainly benefit from the growth in CPU demand. However, due to historical reasons, the LPU business is currently handled by competitors, and investors are widely concerned about whether this means Samsung Foundry has finally made a breakthrough in the AI field. How does Taiwan Semiconductor view this? Does Taiwan Semiconductor have plans to regain this LPU business, and how will the company plan for future inference chip business from customers? Please share your thoughts, thank you. Speaker (Summary): Alright, Charles’s second question is very specific, involving a particular product from a specific customer—typically, we do not comment on such matters. He wants to know how we view the fact that this customer’s LPU product is currently manufactured by our competitor and whether we have plans to regain this LPU business in the future. C.C. Wei: Charles, I think Jeff has given me enough hints—a very specific customer, a very specific product. Let me directly answer your question: we are working with this customer on their next-generation product. We are highly confident in our technological capabilities and will do everything possible to secure every potential business opportunity. Is that acceptable? Charles Shi: Excellent, thank you, C.C. That was a very valuable response. Thank you very much. Jeff Su: Thank you, Charles. Thank you, C.C. Thank you, Wendell. With this, our Q&A session for today has officially concluded. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is a leading pure-play semiconductor foundry that provides wafer fabrication and related services to the global semiconductor industry. title: AI computing power demand is overwhelming everything! Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM.US) reported a 58% surge in quarterly profits, with the AI 'spending battle' still in full swing. - Moomoo
content: To create a seamless experience and for website quality and improvement purposes, we use cookie technologies for verification and quality purposes, to personalize the content presented, and to provide advertising that may be of specific interest to you. Learn our Privacy Policy here. 这一AI算力产业链的最新数据动态，可谓表明新一轮的中东地缘政治冲突并未压制全球蓬勃发展的AI算力基础设施投资以及全球范围持续井喷式扩张的AI算力需求。 智通财经APP获悉，有着“全球芯片代工之王”称号的英伟达和苹果公司最核心芯片代工商台积电(TSM.US)公布的最新季度业绩显示，第一季度净利润疯狂飙升58%，这一AI算力产业链的最新数据动态，可谓表明新一轮的中东地缘政治冲突并未压制全球蓬勃发展的AI算力基础设施投资以及全球范围持续井喷式扩张的AI算力需求。台积电在截至3月的第一季度实现净利润高达5,725亿新台币(约180亿美元)，显著高于分析师们平均预期的5,424亿新台币——这一预期自2月以来可谓不断被分析师们上调。 市场重点聚焦的业绩展望方面，台积电管理层给出的预期指引可谓全面碾压近期不断被上修的市场平均预期。台积电预计今年总营收增长将超过30%，高于此前低于30%的预期以及25%-28%的市场普遍预期;该公司高管还表示，鉴于AI芯片以及先进封装需求仍然非常强劲，资本支出将接近此前预测区间(最高560亿美元)的上限，并且强调未来3年资本支出将显著高于过去3年，这表明他们对未来增长前景可谓充满信心。 台积电还预计第二季度营收区间390亿美元至402亿美元，显著高于市场预期的381亿美元;预计第二季度毛利率65.5%至67.5%，同样高于市场预估的64%。 值得注意的是，这些业绩展望自1月底开始谷歌、亚马逊、英伟达等美国超级科技巨头们公布强劲业绩以来可谓不断被上调，即使这样台积电给出的官方展望仍然强于不断上修后的分析师预期，足以见得全球AI GPU/AI ASIC技术路线的AI算力基础设施需求乃至AI数据中心建设浪潮带来的高性能网络芯片与定制化存储主控芯片需求究竟有多么炸裂，台积电业绩增长轨迹仍将处于自2024年以来的“英伟达式”炸裂式增长。 台积电第一季度营收则同比增长35%，同样显著强于市场预期。Q1台积电总营收为1.134万亿元新台币，同比增长35%，高于预估的1.12万亿元新台币;营业利润为6590亿元新台币，亦超出分析师们普遍预估的6238亿元新台币。从制程角度来看，先进制程依然是台积电营收的核心引擎。5nm级别制程营收占比最高，达36%;3nm制程紧随其后，占比25%，合计5nm及以下制程贡献超过60%的营收份额，并呈现逐季提速的增长趋势，凸显出AI芯片制程愈发倾向于3nm及以下最先进制程。 毛利率堪称是台积电第一季度业绩的最大亮点。台积电Q1毛利率升至创纪录的66.2%，较上季度的62.3%扩张近4个百分点，大幅超出市场平均预期的64.5%;营业利益率则大幅升至58.1%，高于市场预期的55.6%及上季度的54.0%，凸显盈利结构在AI算力炸裂式需求之下的持续扩张态势。 台积电的最新业绩无疑将有助于平息市场对于中东地缘政治危机局势带来高油价冲击将削弱极高建设成本且高能AI数据中心，甚至削弱iPhone等消费电子设备需求的担忧情绪。这场战争已对全球航运路线和能源价格造成压力，投资者们正在积极寻找线索，以判断其影响是否会蔓延至科技巨头们的万亿美元级别史无前例AI算力支出计划。 台积电无比强劲业绩可谓全面凸显出，当模型规模、推理链路与多模态/代理式Agentic AI工作负载推动算力资源消耗呈指数型外扩时，科技巨头们的资本开支主线愈发倾向于向AI算力需求井喷之下的AI算力基础设施集中，全球投资者们更是将围绕英伟达、谷歌TPU集群与AMD的新品迭代与AI算力集群交付预期的“半导体股票牛市叙事”，继续锚定为全球股市中最具确定性的景气投资叙事之一;同时也意味着电力、液冷散热系统、光互连供应链等与AI训练/推理密切相关的投资主题将跟随英伟达、AMD以及博通、台积电、美光等AI算力领军者们在中东地缘政治局势面临不确定性之际，仍继续位列股票市场最火热投资阵营。 来自华尔街最大规模资管巨头贝莱德的股票策略师们已重新转向“超配”美国与新兴市场股票资产，主要因为他们认为新一轮中东地缘政治冲突对全球经济增长造成的实质性质损害“很可能是非常可控的”。在核心投资主题/投资风向方面，贝莱德策略师们重点看好与AI算力基础设施密切相关联的半导体类股票，比如美国股市以及韩国和中国台湾股市的相关AI算力产业链领军者们。 如上图所示的那样，台积电股价因AI热潮触及历史新高——英伟达和大多数其他AI芯片巨头们产能都极度依赖台积电芯片制造体系。 尽管谷歌母公司Alphabet Inc，以及亚马逊、Facebook母公司Meta Platforms Inc.和微软、甲骨文都承诺今年将配置至少约6500亿美元用于AI算力基础设施支出，但是台积电及其英伟达等头部大客户们仍面临一些华尔街分析师质疑，即它们是否能够维持甚至进一步扩张当前的强劲业绩增速，以及这些科技巨头会否因为现金流紧张或者实际云端AI算力资源需求不如此前它们预料得那么乐观而削减支。台积电公布的最新强劲业绩以及未来展望，可谓令这些关于全球AI数据中心新建设与扩建进程以及关于AI算力需求的所有质疑逻辑瞬间瓦解。 在爆炸式销售推动英伟达成为全球市值最高公司并且推动台积电成为亚洲最大实质公司之后，投资者们正寻求各种强有力保证，以确认蓬勃发展的AI算力支出能够持续下去。 在台积电股价今年以来强劲涨势推动之下，中国台湾股市市值升至4.14万亿美元，超越英国跻身全球第七，台积电、鸿海以及纬创等与前所未有的AI数据中心建设狂潮密切相关联的科技公司们可谓持续吸引全球资金流入。 市场还猜测，中东地缘政治危机如果长期持续下去，可能扰乱关键芯片制造零部件以及产量主要集中于中东国家的氦气等关键芯片制造相关气体的供应。设备供应受限也可能限制这条规模可能高达1万亿美元的芯片产业链条的增长，主要因为阿斯麦(ASML Holding NV)、应用材料以及泛林等半导体设备公司无法足够快地扩充产能，以满足包括台积电在内的客户对尖端设备的需求。 对于中东战争带来的供应链影响，台积电管理层在业绩电话会议上可谓缓解了所有投资者担忧，台积电表示正在从不同地区的多个来源采购特种气体，暂时不会受到原材料供给影响。台积电还表示，鉴于中东战争，某些化学品和气体的价格可能有所上涨，但现在量化其对于台积电的影响还为时过早，并且表示氦气和氢气库存仍然充足。 台积电生产着全球绝大多数最为先进制程的芯片，尤其是手握几乎所有5nm及以下先进制程制程芯片以及CoWoS/3D等先进封装产能。鉴于其是英伟达、AMD、博通公司以及苹果公司的最核心芯片代工合作伙伴，在全球竞相建设AI算力基础设施集群的大背景下，近年来台积电成为全球AI热潮的最核心受益群体。今年以来，台积电美股ADR交易价格已上涨约25%，大幅跑赢英伟达等主要客户。 与此同时，该公司也在应对另一项担忧：存储芯片持续短缺——而这并非台积电主要生产的芯片组件，但是存储芯片持续且严峻的供不应求将使全球智能手机市场自2023年以来首次大幅萎缩。这家芯片制造商在2026年初曾表示，其供应的主要是全球最高端AI智能手机，这一市场的需求依然强劲，并且对存储组件涨价的敏感度较低。 随着芯片成为全球各国和各大科技巨头们竞逐史无前例AI大浪潮的核心战略资产，台积电也面临着长期老对手英特尔与三星电子带来的3nm及以下先进制程芯片代工竞争压力，以及试图进入这一炙手可热领域的新挑战者所带来的竞争压力。比如，特斯拉CEO埃隆·马斯克正在推进其雄心勃勃的Terafab大型芯片制造项目，为这位亿万富豪旗下包括特斯拉.、SpaceX和xAI在内的强劲AI算力基础设施需求制造芯片;与此同时，日本政府正在正斥巨资支持Rapidus Corp.，希望这家日本AI初创公司能够在2027年开始生产2nm及以下的最尖端芯片。 台积电这份一季度净利润同比大增58%、营收同比增长35%并超过预期，且业绩展望碾压一切预期的完美成绩，对整个AI算力产业链的积极影响可谓非常明确——即AI需求并没有因为中东战争导致的高油价压力、原材料运力与能源扰动而熄火，反而仍在把先进制程AI芯片与CoWoS先进封装推向满载状态。 同时带有“业绩确定性+高贝塔属性”标签的那些与AI算力基础设施直接相关的股票——即英伟达、台积电以及AMD、博通所领衔的“AI算力超级天团”，往往是整体股票市场或者科技股反弹逻辑里最敏感、最先动且上攻幅度也最大的一层。背后的核心逻辑可谓极其“硬核”：这一层直接绑定的是科技巨头们持续创纪录的AI资本开支，而不是单纯讲故事。 AI hyperscalers(即谷歌、微软以及亚马逊等超级云计算巨头们) 仍在延续资本开支军备竞赛，只要它们“更愿意举债和裁员，也不愿在AI capex 竞赛中(即所谓的“AI算力军备竞赛”)选择退缩”，整个AI算力产业链条的领军者们就仍有配置价值。 对全球股票市场和AI算力产业链配置而言，这份业绩最重要的信号无疑在于AI算力超级周期并未在任何程度上被证伪，反而进一步收敛到少数真正受益的硬核供需瓶颈的核心技术环节。 未来最确定的受益者，仍然是那些卡住全球最先进制程AI芯片供给、先进封装、HBM/eSSD/企业级DRAM存储/企业级服务器CPU、数据中心光互连设备、AI电力链和关键半导体设备等AI算力产业链领军公司们。 美国银行的策略师们近日公布的一份预测数据显示，在全球最核心AI算力产业链领军者们(英伟达、博通、台积电以及迈威尔科技所主导，前瞻估值在15x-20x之间)以及存储/逻辑芯片、2.5D/3D先进封装、数据中心电力链条等领域加速增长的推动下，到2030年，全球半导体市场总规模将达到2万亿美元，2025至2030年的年复合增长预期为20%。相比之下，至少截至2025年，全球半导体市场规模不到1万亿美元。 根据机构汇编的最新分析师预期，亚马逊连同谷歌母公司Alphabet、Facebook母公司Meta Platforms Inc.，以及甲骨文公司和微软，预计将在2026年的累计人工智能相关资本支出达到大约6500亿美元，还有一些分析师认为整体支出可能超过7000亿美元——意味着同比AI资本开支增幅可能超过70%。 值得注意的是，上述的这五大美国超级科技巨头，预计将在2023年至2026年间，为打造无比庞大的AI算力基础设施累计投入约1.5万亿美元;相比之下，这些科技巨头们在2022年之前的整个历史统计期间累计投资约6000亿美元。 The latest data dynamics of the AI computing power industry chain indicate that the new round of geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East has not suppressed global investment in the booming AI computing power infrastructure, nor has it curbed the continuously surging demand for AI computing power worldwide. According to Zhitong Finance, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM.US), the world’s leading chip foundry and core chip manufacturer for NVIDIA and Apple, reported its latest quarterly earnings showing a staggering 58% surge in net profit in the first quarter. This latest development in the AI computing power supply chain indicates that the new round of Middle Eastern geopolitical conflicts has not dampened the vigorous global investment in AI computing infrastructure or the explosive demand for AI computing power worldwide. Taiwan Semiconductor achieved a net profit of NT$572.5 billion (approximately US$18 billion) in the first quarter ended March, significantly surpassing analysts’ average forecast of NT$542.4 billion — a figure that has been consistently revised upward since February. Regarding the market’s focus on earnings guidance, Taiwan Semiconductor's management provided an outlook that comprehensively outpaced the recently upwardly revised market consensus. The company expects total revenue growth this year to exceed 30%, higher than the previous forecast of below 30% and the market’s general expectation of 25%-28%. Company executives further stated that capital expenditures will approach the upper limit of the previously forecast range (up to US$56 billion), given the continued robust demand for AI chips and advanced packaging. They emphasized that capital expenditures over the next three years will be significantly higher than the past three years, reflecting their strong confidence in future growth prospects. Taiwan Semiconductor also forecasted second-quarter revenue to range between US$39 billion and US$40.2 billion, significantly higher than the market expectation of US$38.1 billion. The gross margin for the second quarter is expected to be between 65.5% and 67.5%, also surpassing the market estimate of 64%. Notably, these forecasts have been continuously revised upward since late January when major U.S. tech giants like Google, Amazon, and NVIDIA announced strong earnings. Even so, Taiwan Semiconductor’s official guidance remains stronger than the upwardly revised analyst estimates, underscoring the explosive demand for AI GPU/AI ASIC technology-based computing infrastructure and the surging need for high-performance networking chips and customized storage controller chips driven by the wave of AI data center construction. Taiwan Semiconductor’s growth trajectory will continue to follow the “NVIDIA-like” explosive expansion since 2024. Taiwan Semiconductor’s first-quarter revenue grew by 35% year-over-year, also significantly exceeding market expectations. Total Q1 revenue reached NT$1.134 trillion, a 35% increase year-over-year, surpassing the estimated NT$1.12 trillion. Operating profit was NT$659 billion, also exceeding analysts’ consensus estimate of NT$623.8 billion. From a process perspective, advanced processes remain the core driver of Taiwan Semiconductor’s revenue. Revenue from 5nm-class processes accounted for the highest share at 36%, followed closely by 3nm processes at 25%. Combined, 5nm and below processes contributed over 60% of revenue and exhibited accelerating quarter-on-quarter growth, highlighting the increasing trend toward 3nm and below cutting-edge processes for AI chips. Gross margin was the standout highlight of Taiwan Semiconductor’s Q1 performance. The Q1 gross margin surged to a record 66.2%, expanding nearly 4 percentage points from the previous quarter’s 62.3% and significantly surpassing the market’s average forecast of 64.5%. Operating margin rose sharply to 58.1%, higher than the market expectation of 55.6% and the previous quarter’s 54.0%, reflecting the sustained expansion of profitability under the explosive demand for AI computing power. Taiwan Semiconductor’s latest results will undoubtedly help alleviate market concerns that the Middle Eastern geopolitical crisis and rising oil prices could weaken investments in high-cost, energy-intensive AI data centers or even dampen demand for consumer electronics like iPhones. The conflict has already pressured global shipping routes and energy prices, prompting investors to actively seek clues on whether its impact might spill over into the unprecedented trillion-dollar AI computing expenditure plans of tech giants. Taiwan Semiconductor’s exceptionally strong performance underscores that as model sizes, inference chains, and multimodal/Agentic AI workloads drive exponential increases in computing resource consumption, tech giants are increasingly channeling their capital expenditures toward AI computing infrastructure amid the explosive demand for AI capabilities. Global investors are anchoring their focus on the “semiconductor stock bull narrative,” centered around NVIDIA, Google TPU clusters, and AMD’s product iterations and AI computing cluster delivery expectations, positioning it as one of the most certain growth narratives in global equity markets. It also implies that investment themes closely tied to AI training and inference—such as power systems, liquid cooling solutions, optical interconnect supply chains—will continue to rank among the hottest investment areas in the stock market, following leaders like NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor, and Micron, despite uncertainties surrounding the Middle Eastern geopolitical situation. Equity strategists from Blackrock, the largest asset management giant on Wall Street, have shifted back to an “overweight” stance on U.S. and emerging market equities, primarily because they believe the actual economic damage caused by the new round of Middle Eastern geopolitical conflicts “is likely to be very manageable.” Regarding core investment themes/trends, Blackrock strategists are particularly bullish on semiconductor stocks closely linked to AI computing infrastructure, including key players in the AI computing supply chain listed on U.S., South Korean, and Taiwanese stock exchanges. As shown in the chart above, Taiwan Semiconductor’s stock price hit a record high amid the AI boom — with NVIDIA and most other AI chip giants heavily reliant on Taiwan Semiconductor’s manufacturing ecosystem. The robust rise of Taiwan Semiconductor and its market capitalization expansion have propelled Taiwan's stock market value to surpass that of the UK for the first time. Despite commitments from Alphabet Inc., Amazon, Meta Platforms Inc., Microsoft, and Oracle to allocate at least approximately $650 billion this year for AI computing infrastructure expenditures, Taiwan Semiconductor and its leading customers like NVIDIA still face skepticism from some Wall Street analysts. These analysts question whether they can maintain or even further expand their current strong earnings growth rates, and whether these tech giants might cut spending due to cash flow constraints or because actual cloud-based AI computing resource demand is not as optimistic as previously anticipated. Taiwan Semiconductor’s latest robust financial results and forward guidance have effectively dispelled all doubts regarding the pace of global AI data center construction and expansion, as well as concerns about AI computing demand. Following the explosive sales that propelled NVIDIA to become the world’s most valuable company and Taiwan Semiconductor to emerge as Asia’s largest enterprise, investors are now seeking strong assurances that the flourishing expenditure on AI computing capabilities will persist. Driven by Taiwan Semiconductor’s robust stock price performance this year, Taiwan's stock market capitalization has risen to $4.14 trillion, surpassing the UK to rank seventh globally. Tech companies such as Taiwan Semiconductor, Hon Hai Precision Industry, and Wistron, closely associated with the unprecedented wave of AI data center construction, continue to attract substantial global capital inflows. The market also speculates that a prolonged geopolitical crisis in the Middle East could disrupt the supply of critical chip manufacturing components and gases, including helium, whose production is concentrated in Middle Eastern countries. Limited equipment supply may also constrain the growth of this semiconductor supply chain, potentially valued at up to $1 trillion, primarily because companies like ASML Holding NV, Applied Materials, and Lam Research cannot expand capacity quickly enough to meet customer demand for cutting-edge equipment, including from Taiwan Semiconductor. Regarding supply chain impacts from the Middle Eastern conflict, Taiwan Semiconductor's management alleviated all investor concerns during the earnings call. The company stated it is sourcing specialty gases from multiple suppliers across different regions and will not be affected by raw material supply issues in the short term. Taiwan Semiconductor also noted that while prices of certain chemicals and gases may increase due to the Middle Eastern conflict, it is too early to quantify the impact on the company. It added that helium and hydrogen inventories remain sufficient. Taiwan Semiconductor produces the vast majority of the world’s most advanced process chips, particularly holding nearly all 5nm and below advanced process chips and advanced packaging capacities like CoWoS/3D. As the core foundry partner for NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, and Apple, Taiwan Semiconductor has become a central beneficiary of the global AI boom amid the race to build AI computing infrastructure clusters. Year-to-date, Taiwan Semiconductor's ADR trading price has surged approximately 25%, significantly outperforming key customers like NVIDIA. At the same time, the company is addressing another concern: an ongoing shortage of memory chips — which are not among Taiwan Semiconductor's primary products. However, the persistent and severe undersupply of memory chips is expected to cause the global smartphone market to contract significantly for the first time since 2023. This chipmaker indicated earlier in 2026 that its focus remains on supplying the highest-end AI smartphones globally, where demand remains robust and sensitivity to memory component price increases is relatively low. As chips become a core strategic asset in the unprecedented global AI wave pursued by nations and major tech giants alike, Taiwan Semiconductor also faces competitive pressures from long-standing rivals Intel and Samsung Electronics in the arena of 3nm and below advanced process chip manufacturing, as well as new challengers attempting to enter this highly sought-after field. For instance, Tesla CEO Elon Musk is advancing his ambitious Terafab large-scale chip manufacturing project to produce chips for the robust AI computing infrastructure needs of his companies, including Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI. Meanwhile, the Japanese government is investing heavily in Rapidus Corp., hoping this Japanese AI startup can begin producing 2nm and below cutting-edge chips by 2027. Middle Eastern conflicts cannot halt the AI boom! The global AI arms race led by Google, Meta, and Microsoft is far from over. Taiwan Semiconductor's Q1 results, which showed a 58% year-over-year surge in net profit, a 35% increase in revenue exceeding expectations, and an outlook that surpasses all forecasts, have delivered a clear and positive impact on the entire AI computing power supply chain. This demonstrates that AI demand has not been dampened by the high oil price pressures, raw material transport capacity issues, or energy disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict. Instead, it continues to drive advanced process AI chips and CoWoS advanced packaging to full capacity. Stocks directly tied to AI computing infrastructure—those carrying both 'earnings certainty' and 'high beta' attributes, including NVIDIA, Taiwan Semiconductor, AMD, and Broadcom-led 'AI Computing Power Elite Group'—are often the most sensitive, fastest-moving, and highest-gaining layer in the logic of a broader stock market or tech sector rebound. The core rationale behind this is exceptionally robust: this layer is directly tied to record-breaking AI capital expenditures by tech giants, rather than mere speculative narratives. AI hyperscalers (such as Google, Microsoft, and Amazon) continue their arms race in capital expenditure. As long as they prefer taking on more debt or cutting jobs rather than retreating from the AI capex race (the so-called 'AI computing power arms race'), leading players in the AI computing power supply chain will remain valuable for allocation. For global equity markets and AI computing power supply chain positioning, the most significant signal from these earnings is undoubtedly that the AI computing supercycle has not been disproven in any way but instead has further narrowed down to a few core technology bottlenecks with truly hard-core supply-demand dynamics. The most certain beneficiaries in the future will still be those companies at the forefront of supplying the world’s most advanced process AI chips, advanced packaging, HBM/eSSD/enterprise DRAM storage/enterprise server CPUs, data center optical interconnect equipment, AI power chains, and critical semiconductor equipment within the AI computing power supply chain. A recent forecast published by strategists at Bank of America shows that, driven by the accelerated growth of key leaders in the global AI computing power supply chain (led by NVIDIA, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor, and Marvell Technology, with forward valuations ranging between 15x-20x), as well as sectors such as memory/logic chips, 2.5D/3D advanced packaging, and data center power chains, the total size of the global semiconductor market is expected to reach $2 trillion by 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 20% from 2025 to 2030. In contrast, the global semiconductor market size will remain below $1 trillion at least until 2025. According to the latest analyst forecasts compiled by institutions, Amazon, along with Alphabet, the parent company of Google, Meta Platforms Inc., the parent company of Facebook, Oracle Corporation, and Microsoft, is expected to reach a cumulative AI-related capital expenditure of approximately USD 650 billion by 2026. Some analysts believe that total spending could exceed USD 700 billion—implying a year-on-year increase in AI capital expenditures of more than 70%. Notably, these five major U.S. super tech giants are projected to cumulatively invest around USD 1.5 trillion between 2023 and 2026 to build an incredibly vast AI computing power infrastructure. By comparison, these tech giants invested approximately USD 600 billion cumulatively during the entire historical period prior to 2022. title: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Ltd. (NYSE:TSM) Receives Consensus Rating of "Buy" from Brokerages - MarketBeat
content:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Ltd. (NYSE:TSM - Get Free Report) has earned an average recommendation of "Buy" from the fifteen brokerages that are presently  covering the firm, Marketbeat Ratings reports. Two analysts have rated the stock with a hold recommendation, ten have given a buy recommendation and three have assigned  a strong buy recommendation to  the company. The average 1 year price target among brokerages that have issued ratings on the stock in the last year is $391.4286. A number of research analysts have recently issued reports on TSM shares. The Goldman Sachs Group  restated a "buy" rating on shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing in a research report on Monday, January 5th. Weiss Ratings upgraded shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing from a "hold (c+)" rating to a "buy (b-)" rating in a research report on Friday, March 20th. Wedbush  restated an "outperform" rating on shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing in a report on Wednesday, February 11th. Dbs Bank raised shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing to a "moderate buy" rating in a research report on Friday, January 23rd. Finally, Zacks Research downgraded Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing from a "strong-buy" rating to a "hold" rating in a report on Monday, March 16th. Shares of TSM stock opened at $375.97 on Thursday. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing has a 1-year low of $145.84 and a 1-year high of $390.20. The company has a current ratio of 2.62, a quick ratio of 2.42 and a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.17. The stock has a market cap of $1.95 trillion, a price-to-earnings ratio of 35.30, a PEG ratio of 1.16 and a beta of 1.35. The stock has a 50-day moving average of $353.69 and a 200-day moving average of $320.70. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM - Get Free Report) last posted its quarterly earnings data on Tuesday, February 10th. The semiconductor company reported $3.11 EPS for the quarter. The company had revenue of $30.65 billion for the quarter. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing had a return on equity of 34.89% and a net margin of 45.13%. On average, equities research analysts forecast that  Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing will post 9.2 earnings per share for the current year. The firm also recently announced a quarterly dividend, which will be paid on Thursday, July 9th. Investors of record on Thursday, June 11th will be paid a dividend of $0.9503 per share. The ex-dividend date of this dividend is Thursday, June 11th. This represents a $3.80 annualized dividend and a yield of 1.0%. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's dividend payout ratio (DPR) is currently 27.89%. The shares were purchased at an average cost of $55.93 per share, with a total value of $55,930.00. Following the completion of the purchase, the vice president owned 9,051 shares in the company, valued at $506,222.43. This represents a 12.42% increase in their ownership of the stock. The transaction was disclosed in a document filed with the Securities & Exchange Commission, which is available at the SEC website. Insiders own  1.11% of the company's stock. Hedge funds have recently modified their holdings of the stock. Quattro Advisors LLC bought a new stake in  Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing in the fourth quarter valued at $25,000.  Hilton Head Capital Partners LLC bought a new position in shares of  Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing during the 4th quarter worth about $27,000.  Stephens Consulting LLC grew its stake in shares of  Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing by 82.0% in the 4th quarter. Stephens Consulting LLC now owns 91 shares of the semiconductor company's stock valued at $28,000 after buying an additional 41 shares during the period.  Strategic Advocates LLC grew its stake in shares of  Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing by 62.1% in the 4th quarter. Institutional investors own  16.51% of the company's stock. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is a leading pure-play semiconductor foundry that provides wafer fabrication and related services to the global semiconductor industry. Founded in 1987 by Morris Chang and headquartered in Hsinchu, Taiwan, TSMC manufactures integrated circuits on behalf of fabless and integrated device manufacturers, offering contract chip production across a broad set of technologies and products. TSMC's service offering covers logic and mixed-signal process technologies, specialty processes for radio-frequency, power management and embedded memory, and advanced nodes used in mobile, high-performance computing and AI applications. title: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) Q1 2026 Earnings Preview: What Wall Street Forecasts - MEXC
content: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) prepares to unveil its first-quarter 2026 financial results this Thursday, April 16, during pre-market hours. The announcement carries significant weight across the semiconductor industry, reflecting TSMC’s position as the globe’s dominant foundry chipmaker. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, TSM The foundry giant has already provided preliminary revenue data. Last Friday, TSMC disclosed first-quarter sales totaling 1.13 trillion New Taiwan Dollars—approximately $35.76 billion—representing a 35% year-over-year increase that exceeded Wall Street projections. This advance disclosure has established an optimistic backdrop for Thursday’s comprehensive earnings announcement. Financial analysts anticipate earnings per share of $3.30, marking growth exceeding 50% compared to the same quarter in 2025. The revenue consensus stands at $35.35 billion, though the previously disclosed sales figure has already surpassed this threshold. Stefan Chang of Aletheia Capital established a fresh industry-leading price objective of $600, elevated from a previous $500 target, while reaffirming his Buy recommendation. Chang highlighted TSMC’s aggressive capacity buildout initiatives and accelerated deployment of cutting-edge chip packaging solutions. His analysis anticipates the majority of additional production capability becoming operational during 2027 and 2028, with immediate-term sequential revenue expansion projected between 8% and 10%. Haas Liu from Bank of America similarly increased his price objective to NT$2,530 from NT$2,360, maintaining his Buy stance. Liu emphasized robust appetite for high-performance computing processors and artificial intelligence chips, forecasting Q2 revenue growth of 7% to 9% on a sequential basis. Every analyst currently covering the stock—seven in total tracked by Visible Alpha—advises purchasing shares. The mean price target of $423.50 suggests potential appreciation of approximately 14.6% from present trading levels. TSM shares have climbed more than 20% since the beginning of the year and have surged over 137% across the trailing twelve months. Options market activity indicates traders are preparing for TSMC stock volatility of roughly 4.83% to 5% in either direction following the quarterly disclosure. Using Monday’s closing price as a baseline, this positions the upward target near $386—approaching February peak levels—while the downside zone sits around $353. Wedbush analysts observed Friday that the robust first-quarter sales performance reinforces sustained artificial intelligence demand trends. They additionally highlighted the figures as potentially favorable indicators for TSMC’s two largest clients, Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL). The company’s projected first-quarter earnings stand at 20.73 New Taiwan Dollars, equating to roughly 65 cents per American depositary share. TipRanks assigns TSMC a Strong Buy consensus rating, derived from six Buy recommendations and one Hold rating issued within the past three months.
